Nice! I like it. In fact, I created a couple of other related issues ( #1053 and #1054 ) to extend this further in future PRs.

I think the next step would be to add tests for this.

@tiangolo Sorry for the late comeback. I am trying to add documentation and tests for this. Is there any recommendation for them? Shall I just add a section inside `/tutorial/bigger-applications`, or just modify the existing "users" example to accommodate common tags?

And, I did not realize there was a way to add tags when you `include_router` like this: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/bigger-applications/#include-an-apirouter-with-a-prefix-tags-responses-and-dependencies
Would you still want to implement the current PR?

Thanks!

I remember implementing this and begun doing it at that level also before @tiangolo asked to put it in the include_router.
Maybe the tests wrote on that occasion could be a good start
https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/40fe0a8960c5dd32d0a7d1dbbaee253f7372b0ed/tests/test_tags.py

@jbkoh so the use case I could see for allowing the APIRouter to do it is that it makes the APIRouter responsible for telling fastapi it's tags, rather than it being configuration.  The main router should surely be responsible for prefixes and some other options.  My questions to @tiangolo would be if this is accepted, should the old method then be deprecated (initially via warning) otherwise we've got 2 ways to do the same thing which could be confusing.

Thanks for the patience!

I wanted to include this feature with several other shared parameters. And it all had quite some tricky logic and testing. I just finished adding it in #2434.

So it is now supported in FastAPI version 0.62.0 :tada: 

Given that, I'm gonna close this one. But thanks for the effort! :coffee: :cake:

title: Admit containers of pydantic models as responses models
body:
This PR addresses https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/628.

Fixing the immediate problem only required removing a brittle [runtime type check](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/9c3c9b6e78768374868d690bc05918d58481e880/fastapi/routing.py#L300-L302) of each `model` field in a `responses` arg (passed to [`APIRoute.__init__`](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/9c3c9b6e78768374868d690bc05918d58481e880/fastapi/routing.py#L204)). Putting in a more robust version seemed to lead inexorably to making changes in a few other places.

The same type checking effectively happens in parts of the code that accept a `type` and returns a `pydantic.ModelField`. As it turns out, that logic is repeated in a number of places:
- [Processing any `model` fields](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/9c3c9b6e78768374868d690bc05918d58481e880/fastapi/routing.py#L304-L323) in the `responses` arg to `APIRoute.__init__`
- Doing [the same](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/9c3c9b6e78768374868d690bc05918d58481e880/fastapi/routing.py#L246-L264) for the `response_model` arg to `APIRoute.__init__`
- At various places in `fastapi.dependencies.utils`: [here](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/9c3c9b6e78768374868d690bc05918d58481e880/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L365-L388), [here](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/9c3c9b6e78768374868d690bc05918d58481e880/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L697-L718), [here](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/9c3c9b6e78768374868d690bc05918d58481e880/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L757-L767)

Given that, it seemed best to factor out that logic into a standalone function, for the sake of [DRYness](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Don%27t_repeat_yourself). That also makes it easier to raise `fastapi`-specific exceptions, with clearer error messages, when invalid types are passed. And it means that the `model` args in `responses` and `model_field` undergo the same type checking.

Files seem to be named after possible test scenarios, but there are often multiple files testing the same scenario (e.g. `tests/test_additional_response*`). I'm happy to rearrange them, please just LMK how.

> Those additional responses will be included in the OpenAPI schema, so they will also appear in the API docs.

Yeah, the main tests are for the examples in the docs. And then there are extra tests to cover what is not already covered in those. So, it's indeed quite mixed. But it's fine, I just updated them a bit :slightly_smiling_face:
Yep, that's correct. I just updated it a bit to keep the style of the tests consistent with the tests in other places.

title: Added missing unit test dependencies to pyproject.toml
body: The [contribution instructions from the doc](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/contributing/) led to a number of unit tests failing on against a clean venv because of a few missing dependencies. I've read discussions regarding the possible removal of ujson (#820) and aiofiles (encode/starlette#480, encode/starlette#819) as dependencies, so I'm not entirely sure whether we're going to be keeping those in the future but, for the time being, their absence causes the test suite to fail.

title: Moved endpoint execution outside of get_request_handler for profiling
body: At the reccomendation of @tiangolo in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/701#issuecomment-585319420, I have moved the endpoint execution section from `routing.get_request_handler.<locals>.app` to a separate function (currently named `_run_endpoint`) in order to facilitate profiling. Internal functions and callable object properties make it difficult for profilers to identify the function object being called, and since each request is only associated to one endpoint (not counting dependencies), this makes possible to simply profile "the endpoit that was used" for any given request.

Hi @alvarogf97 , thanks for your contribution! :nerd_face: 

The problem with this approach is that it would not have into account dependencies, possibly handling authentication, etc.

I think it could be useful for some specific use cases that don't have dependencies and that probably return the same data for the same parameters in the request. For example, requests that don't change depending on current user logged in, other dependencies, changes in DB, etc.

Because of that, I would prefer not to have it in the docs like that as it would imply that it could "just work" for all the use cases while it's a delicate and possibly dangerous optimization. So I'm going to pass on this. But thanks for your interest and contribution! :tada: :cake: 

Now that the conversation is in GitHub, I'm pretty sure that other users that really need that type of optimization will be able to find it there. :memo:

title: Do not deploy netlify on external prs
body: Do not deploy netlify on external prs. As requested by @tiangolo
comments: Thanks! :+1:

Adding more context about this, FastAPI has been using more Netlify building minutes than the free quota, and Netlify has been charging me for that.

To avoid that cost I set up Netlify deployments using GitHub actions: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/983

To deploy, it needs Netlify credentials that are passed as environment variables to the GitHub action. The credentials are stored in GitHub secrets in the repo, but those secrets are not passed to the actions triggered by PRs from forks: https://github.community/t5/GitHub-Actions/Make-secrets-available-to-builds-of-forks/td-p/30678

So, currently, there's no way to deploy a preview to Netlify using a simple action like that. Then I modified the action to avoid exiting with an error: https://github.com/nwtgck/actions-netlify/pull/33

And that's the action temporarily used here.

A better future (but more involved) alternative, at least that I can think of now, is to create an action that runs periodically, like https://github.com/tiangolo/issue-manager, and that checks for each open PR, and checks if it already wrote a comment with a Netlify deployment for the latest commit of that PR. If not, it would pull that PR, build it, deploy to Netlify, and add a comment with the deployment. This version would work because it's always running from `master`, not from a branch in a fork. But it wouldn't be able to use the default triggers (`on pull_request`, etc).

title: convert json schema const to enum for openapi
body:
OpenAPI does not support `const`, but JSON schema output by pydantic
can include it. `const` is just syntactic sugar for a single element enum,
so let's convert it instead of just dropping it.

ref: https://json-schema.org/understanding-json-schema/reference/generic.html#constant-values

> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1059?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [f2bd2c4...2fc2f7e](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1059?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).
Thanks for your contribution @jashandeep-sohi ! :nerd_face: :rocket: 

The problem is that `root_validator` is only available in Pydantic `1.x`.

FastAPI is still backwards compatible with Pydantic `0.32.x` and there are still too many downloads for those versions (about 10 times more than for 1.x), so I can't drop support for Pydantic < 1.x yet.

---

On the other side, OpenAPI is based on JSON Schema Draft `00`, which is now a bit old. There are a couple of other small inconsistencies between newer JSON Schema versions (provided by Pydantic) and OpenAPI's schemas, but having schemas declared with modern JSON Schema allows some tools to use the generated JSON Schema directly, like https://github.com/rjsf-team/react-jsonschema-form . So, I have preferred to keep the generated recent JSON Schema versions even though there are a couple of inconsistencies in some specific corner cases.

And on the other side, if strict OpenAPI compliance is required, it's also easy to update the Pydantic model to use an enum of a single value instead of a `Literal`. That's also backwards compatible with other versions of Python, given `Literal` is only in Python 3.8.

So I'll pass on this one. But thanks for your contribution and interest! :cake: 
For anyone stumbling on this, you can implement using the `schema_extra` within your base model (as eluded to in Tiangolo's comment):

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel

class MyBaseModel(BaseModel):
    class Config:
        @staticmethod
        def schema_extra(schema, model) -> None:
            for name, prop in schema.get('properties', {}).items():
                if 'const' in prop:
                    prop['enum'] = [prop['const']]
```

also note that this will override the enum, to support the usecase of using a Literal to reference an Enum.

Tested on Pydantic==1.5.1, FastAPI==0.54.1
I have solved the problem of including consts another way in a program of mine. I think this is compatible with older versions of Pydantic and renders well with the current OpenAPI version. Pretty much, if a `Field("value", const=True)` is defined, then `{"const": "value", "example": "value"}` is added to the openapi json, and the schema and example for the types will look correct.

If there is interest in the feature (I think it's badly missing) I can propose a MR doing this in the proper place.

Reference: https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/issues/1666 

```python
class FastAPISubclass(FastAPI):
    def openapi(self) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        if self.openapi_schema:
            return self.openapi_schema

        self.openapi_schema = schema = get_openapi(
            # ...
        )

        # Find the fields with a const value
        for model in self._iter_models():
            for field in model.__fields__.values():
                if not field.field_info.const:
                    continue
                try:
                    props = schema["components"]["schemas"][model.__name__]
                    props = props["properties"][field.name]
                except KeyError:
                    continue
                props["const"] = props["example"] = field.default

        return schema

    def _iter_models(self):
        seen = set()
        for route in self.router.routes:
            model = getattr(route, "response_model", None)
            if hasattr(model, "__fields__"):
                if model not in seen:
                    yield model
                    seen.add(model)

            if getattr(route, "body_field", None) is not None:
                model = route.body_field.type_
                if model not in seen:
                    yield model
                    seen.add(model)
```

title: Chinese translation
body: FastAPI文档index部分中文翻译
comments:
I don't even know if the documentation even supports having translations with the way is is setup right now; not to mention that translating all of it would be a huge endeavor. I know FastAPI has contributors from a variety of countries speaking a variety of languages, but I guess English has done a good enough job at getting everyone involved to understand each other so far, so it's not a priority for anyone at the moment.

EDIT: Nevermind, hadn't noticed #1055 yet.
review_comments: Linking to a 3rd party image that may change any time doesn't seem like a good idea.
I'm not sure if this translation is very good, FastAPI is not an API but a framework to build APIs, and the translation is quite much more informal than the original text.
Shouldn't this rather be 自动完成?
看到有人这么做 doesn't convey very well the point of someone building what Hug was supposed to be.
Not sure why the past tense in the former part of the sentence.
Thanks for your comments.  Recently, I learned a lot about Github.

title: Export utils from Starlette
body:
:sparkles: Export utils from Starlette. :star: 

Re-export most of the relevant Starlette utils for FastAPI.

I myself some times have a hard time remembering which things to import from `fastapi` and which from `starlette`. This simplifies that, it's just for developer experience. I also added notes everywhere explaining that it all really comes from Starlette, and that this is all just/mostly syntax sugar.

As I'm now re-exporting most of the middlewares (except the ones that are not directly relevant for FastAPI, where FastAPI provides a "preferred" alternative), that required extra tests. ...So, I added extra docs with examples, to test them :nerd_face: :smile: 

The same with responses and WSGI middleware.

This will also allow providing extra things in FastAPI like the future `ORJSONResponse`, without generating more confusion about some parts imported from Starlette and some from FastAPI.

title: Fix exclude_unset and aliases in response model validation.
body: Should fix both https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1043 and https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1063, but might come with some performance penalty since unnecessary serialization is being done. Better solutions that pass the given tests are more than welcome, but I don't see a much better way other than recursion in cases where there are models inside basic data structures like lists and dicts.

Is there any slight performance benefit of making this the first if condition?

title: Handle automatic embedding with Depends
body:
Two issues:
1) Whether or not to embed automatically wasn't taking into account the fact that `flat_dependant.body_params` could have repeated fields with the same name (Such as from a dependency chain). Fix: Look at unique names in `flat_dependant.body_params`, and do not embed if there's only one unique name and embed is false.
2) When processing the param body, automatically embedded bodies were sometimes processed incorrectly because we look at `Depends` parameters one-by-one. Ideally we look at ALL body parameters, but this doesn't seem to be available at the time when we process dependency bodies. The fix here is to set the `embed` parameter on field info for all params. (Not sure if there's a better fix).

TODO:
This case is undefined behavior and currently doesn't throw an error but perhaps it should? (Multiple types for the same body)
```
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, Body
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI(title='test')

class Item(BaseModel):
    data: str

def dependency(item: str):
    return item

@app.post("/no-duplicates")
async def no_duplicates(item: Item, item2: Item = Depends(dependency)):
    return [item, item2]
```

This change fixed the issue I am experiencing, @Toad2186 .

>Would it maybe be useful to have a test that validates if the openapi spec looks correct with that change?

This could be a good idea. @tiangolo Are there any existing openapi spec validation tests? I'm not sure how fancy you'd want to get with this though--there's a large variety of scenarios with those.
@tiangolo I would appreciate this fix to be merged for the next release. Due to the issue, FastAPI currently produces an incorrect api spec.

Please help me. I want to build a boat. Which posts the news paper in my channel in automatic pdf format every day. Every morning when the paper is placed on the news paper's website, its pdf file is automatically posted to my channel.

Thanks for your contribution! :cake: :rocket: 

I updated it a bit to include handling sub-dependencies and more than 2 body fields.

Merged! :tada:

@tiangolo this broke my use-case. I have a payload like this:

```json
{
  "field1": "foo",
  "field2": "bar",
  "dep_field": "baz"
}
```

And I've been processing it like this:

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, Body
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI(title='test')

class Form1(BaseModel):
    field1: str
    field2: str

def require_dep_field(dep_field: str = Body(..., embed=True)):
    # do stuff with data here
    return dep_field

@app.post("/form-test")
async def form_test(form: Form1, dep_field: str = Depends(require_dep_field)):
    print(form)
    print(dep_field)
    return "ok"
```

Before, this worked just fine. After this change, I now get an error that `form` is a required field, because obviously this change is forcing it to be `embed=True`.

AFAICT, I can no longer do this (process a part of my request body with a dep while having the rest of the payload be inlined). Am I missing something?

@acnebs if you are still having this problem, please create a new issue for me to be able to check it there as I would lose sight of it here.

> Sorry for the long delay! 🙈  I wanted to personally address each issue/PR and they piled up through time, but now I'm checking each one in order.

title: Export startlette status as a var instead of directly from startlette
body: I'm not sure if this is worth doing but it shouldn't cause any harm(?). Doing the import like this tricks PyCharm into showing `fastapi.status` instead of `startlette.status`...
comments:

> Merging [#1080](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1080?src=pr&el=desc) into [master](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/commit/025b38df40a18f94322fab445aee13024fe783da&el=desc) will **not change** coverage by `%`.
> The diff coverage is `n/a`.

[![Impacted file tree graph](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1080/graphs/tree.svg?width=650&height=150&src=pr&token=llDQVnTfnB)](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1080?src=pr&el=tree)

[Continue to review full report at Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1080?src=pr&el=continue).
> **Legend** - [Click here to learn more](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/codecov-delta)
> `Δ = absolute <relative> (impact)`, `ø = not affected`, `? = missing data`
> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1080?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [025b38d...5acc2f2](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1080?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).

Thanks for your suggestion! :cake: 

I like that it improves editor support, but I'm not totally convinced by the fact that it's kind of a hack. But the main problem is that it wouldn't make sense to do the trick for this and not do it for all the other variables, like Response, Request, the middlewares, etc.

Nevertheless, in the end, it's actually correct, it's the same `status` variable, so there's no harm in using the `status` variable directly from Starlette.

The good thing is that when using things that are exclusive to FastAPI, like `ORJSONResponse`, it knows where to import them from. And when using `HTTPException`, that FastAPI has a custom modified version, it is suggested as well, so I think it's safe to leave it as is.

But thanks for checking it and finding out this behavior! It's interesting to know how that works underneath :nerd_face:

title: Allow subclasses of known types to be encoded with superclass encoder
body:
Implementation of https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/pull/1291 which was closed due to performance concerns, another option was attempted but found to be 50% slower in a related issue here: https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/issues/1157#issuecomment-572450669

I have not seen the same results in benchmarking pydantic, so I wanted to open this here to see if the performance impact is the same.

I have been intending to add these docs for awhile :sweat_smile:  So I updated it with what I had in mind and to include a couple of extra explanations.

Thanks for your contribution! :tada: :cake:
Question to @alexmitelman : any benefit to using Pydantic vs. Starlette's baked-in [configuration stuff](https://www.starlette.io/config)?

> Question to @alexmitelman : any benefit to using Pydantic vs. Starlette's baked-in [configuration stuff](https://www.starlette.io/config)?

IMHO, Starlette solution looks more low level, while using Pydantic you just declare class as usual.
Let's compare:

Starlette
```python
from starlette.config import Config


config = Config(".env")

DEBUG = config('DEBUG', cast=bool, default=False)
```

Pydantic
```python
from pydantic import BaseSettings


class Settings(BaseSettings):
    debug: bool = False

    class Config:
        env_file = '.env'
```

@alexmitelman that's a description of syntax, not a functional differentiation. It also does not really explain the strengths / benefits of that syntactical difference, or if there is one. 

I've now used both. IMO - given FastAPI's reliance on simplicity, I think Starlette's setup should be the preferred structure for the following three reasons:
1. Starlette's `config` does not require the installation of a secondary dependency (Pydantic requires `python-dotenv`).
2. Starlette's `config` caches the variable by default (no need for a custom `lru_cache` implementation).
2. Starlette's suggested [`config.py` file structure](https://www.starlette.io/config) - simple, flat - is consistent with the `.env` file format.

That said - I've very recently taken to using Pydantic for settings management in larger projects for the following three reasons:

1. Pydantic is better for codebases with multiple runtimes (it wouldn't make sense to install Starlette on a worker just to gain access to its' config class for the sake of code sharing).
2. Pydantic lends itself well to the creation of constructed globals (e.g. `DATABASE_URI` as the combination of `DATABASE_PORT`, `DATABASE_HOST`, etc etc).
3. Pydantic has a much larger library of data types to cast / validate globals against.

Those three reasons are compelling (don't get me wrong, Pydantic is amazing; I use it in everything) but I'd imagine the first three would make Starlette's config preferable in most situations.
I was surprised by this change, @leosussan summarized what I needed to know.

title: Rename a function in tutorial
body:
[Path Parameters - FastAPI](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/path-params/#path-convertor)

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/files/{file_path:path}")
async def read_user_me(file_path: str):
    return {"file_path": file_path}
```

I changed the name of the above function from `read_user_me` to `read_file`.

title: Apply openapi_prefix to redirect URL
body:
For my application I need to use Apache as a Reverse Proxy, like this:
```
<VirtualHost *:80>
    ServerName ${SERVER_NAME}
    DocumentRoot ${SERVER_DOCUMENT_ROOT}
    ##  Other Redirects
    
    ## FastApi App
    Redirect permanent /app    ${SERVER_NAME}:/app
</VirtualHost>

<VirtualHost *:443>
    ProxyPass         "/app" "http://localhost:57012"
    ProxyPassReverse  "/app" "http://localhost:57012"

```
In order to do this, I have to setup the fastapi app with the following settings:

```
app = FastAPI(
    title="app",
    description="Ipsum Lorem",
    openapi_prefix="/app",
    swagger_ui_init_oauth={"clientId": "app-id",},
)
```
The openapi_prefix is needed to set up the application behind a reverse proxy as carefully explained in FastApi documentation, otherwise `openapi.json` is not served in the right place. But, for some reason this openapi_prefix was not being applied on the oauth2 redirect_uri in the swagger html generator.
comments:
Travis build was failing because of this:
```
fastapi/applications.py:116: error: Unsupported operand types for + ("str" and "None")
fastapi/applications.py:116: note: Right operand is of type "Optional[str]"
```

I don't really understand how these type checkers work, but it doesn't seem to me that Optional[str] is the appropriate choice, given that swagger_ui_oauth2_redirect_url is never None and has a default value of type str.
Build is still failing and I'm trying to find what is wrong since the 3 first builds don't explicitly say which error was raised.

title: Add notes about using `typing.Dict` inplace of a Pydantic model
body:
There are good recommendations for Models and partial updates with PATCH.
However, I believe it's worth pointing out that `typing.Dict` can be used in place of a pydantic model.

https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/body-updates/#partial-updates-recap
https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/extra-models/

```python
from typing import Dict

@app.patch("/items/{id}")
def patch_item(item_patch: Dict):
```

This PR solves the example issue in #894 as well as a more complicated private project of mine. LGTM 👍
Very clever! Thanks for your contribution @voegtlel :cake: :rocket:

For a complex app that I'm building I've seen memory usage go up from ~140MB to ~305MB and app start up time increased from ~2.4s to ~9.5s when moving from version `0.46.0` to `0.54.1`.

It looks like most of the additional time is spent in `create_cloned_field` which was changed a lot in this PR.

Has anyone else had similar issues?

[PR 1100](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1100) still works for me, removing the issues mentioned above.

I've cherry-picked his commit on top of the current head in this repo (4e77737a3f7bf2608132ea170e9ff013b5af6732) and that works as well.
@jmagnusson I would recommend that you create a new issue, or this might be left unnoticed.

@jmagnusson as @phy25 says, it would probably be better in a new issue.

Anyway, if you want to profile specific functions and timings, you can try https://github.com/sm-Fifteen/asgi-server-timing-middleware

Or the ideas in this thread: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/701

That might help to detect where is the change exactly.

title: Update badge for coverage
body:
:art: Update badge for coverage. Use Shields.io, as Codecov's one shows coverage for the last PR from a fork from `master`.

So, it some times shows 99%, or 40% or different things, if a PR that is not yet merged has a lower coverage.

title: Fix minor grammatical mistakes in the async docs
body:
First of all, 
Thank you for this awesome framework and documentation! I only started using it last week but I'm already in love with it. You provide a lot of context for all the components and decisions and this makes it so easy to follow!

- I think the correct pronoun when referring to a computer is `it`. 💻 
- I also used the adjective `Next` but I think in a few places we could replace "and" and "then" with other conjunctive adverbials ([extensive list here](https://www.citationmachine.net/grammar-and-plagiarism/conjunction/conjunctions-list/)) 🤓 
- The verb tensing should `sit`, `sat`, `sitting` - I've made that mistake myself 🙈 

Let me know what you think 🙇
I hope to start by contributing to the docs and move into helping out with some of the code 👨‍💻

"经过缜密研究后围绕这些标准而设计。并非狗尾续貂。" should be better?

”学习“ should be "复习" or "回顾”
“验证” -> “校验"

missing a space before "Starlette"
"依赖关系“ is incorrect.
所有注入的依赖都可以从请求中获取数据、扩充路径操作的约束条件并自动生成文档
missing “parameters”，“即使在依赖项中被定义的路径操作参数也会被**自动验证**。”
或者换句话说，不需要上面这么复杂，就只是导入并使用你需要的代码。
通过注入依赖，任何的集成都被设计得非常易于使用，你可以用和*路径操作*相同的结构和语法，只用两行代码为你的应用创建一个“插件”。

Better leave a blank line before this line.

It's hard to understand "默认情况下只设置了两个". 补全主语宾语会更容易理解。

因为该框架是基于 OpenAPI，所以有很多可选项，**FastAPI** 默认自带两个交互式 API 文档。
Leave a space before and after `<a href="https://www.jetbrains.com/research/python-developers-survey-2017/#tools-and-features" class="external-link" target="_blank">`

title: Add support for ASGI root_path for openapi docs
body:
ASGI specifies the `root_path` connection scope variable. This is the path the application is mounted under (the equivalent of WSGI's `SCRIPT_NAME`).

This PR ensures that this root is used as a prefix for the openapi.json URL, as a prefix for paths contained in openapi.json and by the oauth2 redirect endpoint to redirect back to the swagger docs.

To override the path the ASGI server provides you can still explicitly set the `openapi_prefix` variable.

Do note that this is a breaking change for applications that overriden `openapi()` themselves.

This should fix/related to https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/829

> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1199?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [6e1cd45...ac517f0](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1199?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).

I guess this PR solves the same issue that I was having (I didn't even know about issue #829). I solved my problem by just adding the prefix to the redirect url. You can see what I did on my proposed PR https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1159. But it seems to me that your solution involves more steps that I may have been missing. Could you explain? 

_I will probably delete https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1159 since it is redundant, now that you've done this 😄_

Great! :rocket: 

Thanks for the patience, it took me a bit of time to review this as I wanted to first re-read the WSGI and ASGI specs to make sure I wasn't missing anything.

And then to test it all with an actual reverse proxy with path prefix stripping (what the `root_path` would be used for).

I refactored it a bit and took the opportunity to deprecate `openapi_prefix` in favor of the new `root_path` :fire: :rocket: 

And I added a bunch of docs to explain how the `root_path` works and to document how to test it all with a real proxy using Traefik.

Thanks for your contribution! :rocket: :tada: :cake: 

This is available in FastAPI `0.56.0`. :cake:

As it has several approvals I'm gonna merge it as it is right now, so that we have the current version live. And if any of you think it can be improved/updated then we can do that in additional PRs on top, and we can continue the discussion in those new PRs.
review_comments: missing “ This follows the Semantic Versioning conventions.”
你应该做的第一件事，是为你正在使用的 FastAPI 指定一个能够正确运行你的应用的最新版本。
FastAPI 遵循语义化版本控制规范，`1.0.0` 以下的任何版本都可能加入不兼容的变更。
FastAPI 也遵循这样的约定：任何 ”PATCH“ 版本变更都是用来修复 bug 和向下兼容的变更。
“重大变更”should be "不兼容变更"

Definition of Convension from Oxford:

a way in which something is usually done.
an agreement between states covering particular matters, especially one less formal than a treaty.
"规范" means "Standard, specification or regulation", that's not the right word for it.
So the meaning of Convension should be "约定，协定，习俗" rather than "规范"
I think you are right.
the "Auto-tuned" is executed by uvicorn's configuration instead of Docker.
现在你在 Docker 容器中有了一个根据当前服务器（和CPU核心的数量）自动优化好的 FastAPI 服务器。
“decrypted ” should be “解密后的”。
”has integration with sth" should be “可以与某物进行集成”。“Traefik 也集成了 Docker” is incorrect.
missing "or use Hypercorn with multiple workers.","或者通过运行多个职程的方式使用 Hypercorn"

It's need to translation word `sveral` will be better?
`根据特定使用情况和使用工具有几种不同的部署方式。`

I think it changes the meaning of the original sentence, missed some words .
It‘s should be:
```
例如，假设你的应用正在使用 `0.45.0` 版本。 
```
“您” and “你” should be unified.
missing space after `HTTP`
Should be better with `运行基于你的镜像的容器`？

I'm afaid I disagree with you. The present perfect is formed using `has/have + past participle`.  We use the present perfect to indicate that an action happened at an unspecified time before now. The Chinese meaning should be "已经做了某事".  Don't know where is the word "可以" comes from?  

Furthermore, Traefik has intergration with `Lets' Encrpyt` already. See [`Feature of Traefix`](https://docs.containo.us/features/#compare)

Three sentences before `etc` should be **Parallel structure** and all three examples are expressing one thing:  All these cumbersome things you might want to do, Docker is done for you. To convey that, the meanings shoud be `You might also want to make sure fine-tune the number of workers.`,  just as the start of the first two sentences.

`但是紧接着 Let's Encrypt 被创造了` sounds a bit odd, although the original text is `But then Let's Encrypt was created`.

I think `但是紧接着 Let's Encrypt 出现了`  is just fine 😃.

If insisting on not missing `created`, I would say `但是紧接着 Let's Encrypt 应运而生` and the context also makes sense:

> We were supposed to do blablabla to acquire these certificates, however, some people decided to create something since the process was `cumbersome` and `expensive`...and then we have Let's Encrypt. 🎉

但是如果你正做着所有这些操作，你可以仅使用 Docker 镜像自动执行它们。

* `all that` should be `所有这些`. I thought Docker image could only `set up some tooling to make sure it is restarted automatically if it stops` at the outset 😅.
* `这些` are unclear in the context. I suggest adding `操作` after `这些`, since `英语多替代，汉语多重复`.
* `might just use` should be `可以仅使用`.
* `does it automatically` refers to `does all that you are doing automatically`. Here should be `自动执行你正做着的所有这些操作` and we can say `自动执行它们`.

title: OpenAPI schemas & response_model_by_alias
body:
Fixed bug which ignored by_alias flag for a response model set on the route. This made openapi schemas appear incorrectly because Pydantic defaults by_alias to True.

Fixes #771
comments:

Regardless the change is valid and below is a test if anyone can run all of this together please do!

```
from starlette.testclient import TestClient

from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

app = FastAPI()

aliased_field = Field(..., alias='dummy_value')


class Foo(BaseModel):
    dummyValue: str = aliased_field


class Bar(BaseModel):
    dummyValue: str = aliased_field


@app.get("/foo", response_model=Foo, response_model_by_alias=False)
def get_foo():
    return {'dummy_value': "1"}


@app.get("/bar", response_model=Bar, response_model_by_alias=True)
def get_bar():
    return {'dummy_value': "1"}


client = TestClient(app)
response = client.get('/openapi.json')
response = response.json()

assert response['components']['schemas']['Foo']['required'] == ['dummyValue'], "by_alias not passed to Pydantic"
assert response['components']['schemas']['Bar']['required'] == ['dummy_value']

```
Are there any updates on getting this PR merged in? The auto-generated documentation ends up being wrong

> Are there any updates on getting this PR merged in? The auto-generated documentation ends up being wrong

branch has conflicts (also CI's formatting check failed), I think that's the reason why it hasn't been merged yet. @rupertcw (or someone else) can you please fix that?

Thanks for the effort @rupertcw ! :coffee: 

And thanks for the discussion everyone!

`response_model_by_alias` is mainly a hack/workaround for quick experiments, mainly because some people were asking for that, but I'm inclined to deprecating and removing it at some point. Its behavior is actually quite strange, and there's a much better way to solve the same problem.

More background on the parameter config here: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1642 and here: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/264

And a longer explanation with example code showing how to resolve it in a proper way in a comment on the original issue here: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/771#issuecomment-836448352

Given that, I'm gonna close this issue now. But thanks for the interest and effort! :nerd_face:

And thanks for the review and comments @cassiobotaro and @Serrones ! :bow: :clap:
review_comments: Talvez poderiamos colocar _Auto completar_ aqui ?

Auto completar seria o verbo, em termos de substantivo, a palavra "completação" é, ao meu ver, a que chega mais próxima (https://www.dicio.com.br/completacao/). Porém eu acho que podemos utilizar "auto completar" como substantivo masculino nesse caso específico. Farei a alteração.

Acredito não ser necessária a vírgula após a palavra `padrões`.
Parece que a sentença ficaria melhor assim:
`Projetado em cima desses padrões após um estudo meticuloso, em vez de uma reflexão breve.`
Acredito não ser necessária a vírgula após a url

O correto não seria `parâmetro`?
Outra coisa, na última frase tem a redundância com a palavra `precisa`:
`...para fazer o que você precisa e para definir a API que você precisa.`
Não seria melhor substituir o último `precisa` para `quer`, ou outro verbo que dê o mesmo significado?
parâmetros

Ficou estranho essa frase `Validação para a maioria (ou todos?) dos **tipos de dados**`
O que acha de `Validação para a maioria dos (ou todos?) **tipos de dados**`
No fim da frase, não seria melhor `bancos de dados relacionais e não-relacionais, etc.`?
Faltou vírgula antes do etc

review_comments:
```suggestion
Por defecto, **FastAPI** retornará las respuestas utilizando una `JSONResponse`, poniendo el contenido que retornas en tu *path operation* dentro de esa `JSONResponse`.
```

```suggestion
Si quieres retornar códigos de estado adicionales además del principal, puedes hacerlo retornando directamente una `Response`, como una `JSONResponse`, y asignar directamente el código de estado adicional.
```

```suggestion
Por ejemplo, digamos que quieres tener una *path operation* que permita actualizar ítems, y retornar códigos de estado HTTP 200 "OK" cuando sea satisfactorio.
```

```suggestion
    Asegurate que la respuesta tenga los datos que quieras, y que los valores sean JSON válidos (si estás usando `JSONResponse`).
```

```suggestion
Si quieres retornar adicionalmente códigos de estado y respuestas directamente, no estarán incluidas en el esquema OpenAPI (documentación de API), porque FastAPI no tiene una manera de conocer de antemano lo que vas a retornar.
```

```suggestion
Utilizará el código de estado por defecto, o el que sea asignado en tu *path operation*.
```

```suggestion
```

```suggestion
Cuando creas una *path operation*, puedes retornar normalmente cualquier dato: un `dict`, una `list`, un modelo Pydantic, modelo de base de datos, etc.
```

```suggestion
Luego, detrás de escenas, pondría esos datos compatibles con JSON (por ejemplo, un `dict`) dentro de una `JSONResponse` que se usaría para enviar la respuesta al cliente.
```

```suggestion
Pero puedes retornar una `JSONResponse` directamente de tu *path operation*.
```

```suggestion
Como **FastAPI** no realiza ningún cambion en la `Response` que tu retornas, debes asegurate que el contenido esta listo para ello.
```

title: add a switch to disable all output docs in fastapi
body: add a switch to disable output docs in the production environment. They can use the system environment variable or use  `disable_all_output_docs` variable  to disable output docs in an explicit way

> **Legend** - [Click here to learn more](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/codecov-delta)
> `Δ = absolute <relative> (impact)`, `ø = not affected`, `? = missing data`
> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1260?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [06e42a4...aa62ac4](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1260?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).

So fastapi never had this `FASTAPT_ENVIRONMENT` before, and I doubt if production equals to no openapi.

Also I am not sure this quick path is worth implementing. You can always implement this in your own application logic by [passing in `openapi_url`, `docs_url`, `redoc_url`](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/metadata/#openapi-url). I think it's encouraged to use Pydantic's `BaseSettings` to do the envvar reading.

@phy25  I think it's worth to implement it. 

Because people always want to use the same code in local environment and production environment

But you are right, the production doesn't mean that we don't need openapi doc

So I can split it into four  environment variables

1. FASTAPI_DISABLE_ALL_DOCS

2. FASTAPI_DISABLE_OPENAPI_DOCS

3. FASTAPI_DISABLE_SWAGGER_DOCS

4. FASTAPI_DISABLE_REDOCS

I think it's more convenient for people that they want to control the docs visibility simply without any extra code
Reference on `pydantic.BaseSettings`: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/settings/#pydantic-settings
@phy25 Actually I didn't get your point about pedantic?

I just think fastapi prefers using pydantic for config - when you read envvars you may eventually want to read .env files. So probably in the first place I don't think this feature is worth implementing.

Anyway it's just my own opinion.
I see, maybe you mean that we can use pydantic-settings to instead of processing the environment manually?

> I just think fastapi prefers using pydantic for config - when you read envvars you may eventually want to read .env files.

@phy25  that's a good way, I will update my code for this PR 

> So probably in the first place I don't think this feature is worth implementing.

Actually, I have already discuss this feature with my friends who are FastAPI's users too, they think they need a simple way to disable the docs output in the production environment without any extra code.

Again, you may have other production-related config change besides this docs settings, and this can be easily implemented with custom app logic. I still don't think this is worth implementing in the core - my humble opinion.

> you may have other production-related config change besides this docs settings, and this can be easily implemented with custom app logic.

@phy25 this is making sense.

But in my humble personal opinion, the openapi doc, the swagger doc is one of the core features in this framework, and it's deeply about the security problem for the doc in production. That's means people need to care about the accessibility in the sever with FastAPI by disableing the doc or using other ways.

Imagine this, I have a couple of servers made by FastAPI. I want to disable the docs in the production environment( most of the people will do this), this means I will get some duplicate code across the codebase, just like this 

```python

class Settings(BaseSettings):
    environment: str = "DEBUG"


setting = Settings()


def create_app():
    if setting.environment == "PRODUCTION":
        app = FastAPI(openapi_url=None, docs_url=None, redoc_url=None)
    else:
        app = FastAPI()
    return app
```

I think it's not a good way to people. Most of the people want to get a simple way to control the security-sensitive features like the doc. I guess this is not a technical problem, but it's a user experience problem.

Thanks for your review, hope to get more ideas from you. Your idea is very useful and important for me! :)

```suggestion
        self.__disable_all_output_docs = disable_all_output_docs
```

nice touch, I'll fix it

I'm not really sure it would help much having by default a single decorator that handles multiple exceptions that could have very different shapes and types.

So I'm gonna pass on this one, but thanks for your effort! :coffee:

title: ✨ Add support for adding multiple examples in request bodies and path, query, cookie, and header params
body:
This PR enables multiple examples in a pick-list style dropdown in the OpenApi Docs a la the 'Request Body Examples' [here](https://swagger.io/docs/specification/describing-request-body/)

For more information and discussion see the issue this PR related to #822 

~~Note, this still needs tests to cover the 3 new lines.~~ Docs and full coverage tests added Sept 4 with 8138ff6

---

Edit by @tiangolo :nerd_face: :point_down: 

Added support for arguments `example` and `examples` in `Body()`, `Path()`, `Query()`, `Cookie()`, `Header()`, handling compatibility (and incompatibilities) between OpenAPI, JSON Schema, and OpenAPI's 3.0.x custom version of JSON Schema :zany_face: :dizzy:

More details about the standards dizzy mix in a comment below, near the end.

body:
Currently unknown query parameters are just ignored by fastapi, I feel that it should be at least possible to change the behaviour to return a validation error also for unknown.

It adresses #1190 

* not yet documented
* not yet any testing
* maybe different path to pass that option
* maybe rename to something else, maybe could be thought to generally
  check unknowns, however for models, pydantic has to be adapted

Is there any progress on this? I see there are some merge conflicts and missing tests. Having this optional feature would be a nice to have for some work I am doing as well

No progress from my side, I'm a bit short on time and before investing into testing and docs I thought to wait for a bit of feedback about the implementation. But I guess with more than 60 pending pull requests it is quite difficult to keep up.
I am interested with the fix, when it will be available.

I'm interested in the fix too. I know it has been considered as an edge case by @tiangolo in a similar issue/PR, but I can explain my niece use case.

I'm implementing the "OGC API - Features - Part 1: Core" (http://docs.opengeospatial.org/is/17-069r3/17-069r3.html) which is a standard for fetching geospatial features over HTTP. In section 7.6 we read the following:

> The server SHALL respond with a response with the status code 400, if the request URI includes a query parameter that is not specified in the API definition.

This is not a big deal, but just wanted to let you guys know of an use case where this functionality would come in handy.

could it be merged?

title: Add optional custom Swagger css/js/favicon urls for docs.
body:
I opened this PR to allow the optional passing of a custom CSS url as a theme, as there are many such themes available. http://ostranme.github.io/swagger-ui-themes/

I didn't want to clutter up the named args in the `FastAPI` constructor, so I am just testing to see if the argument exists in the `self.extra` dictionary. Let me know what you think, if you'd like changes to the PR for the functionality, or aren't willing to accept.

Thanks again for the great work in this project, I really enjoy it!
comments:
Thanks for your interest!

I think that for additional UI modifications it would be best to add a new path operation with them, as described here: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/extending-openapi/#create-a-path-operation-to-test-it

It's fairly easy to add a custom path operation with a new UI, and supporting all the possible combinations of additional customizations to the UI would become difficult.

So I'll pass on this one. But thanks for your effort and contribution! :coffee: :cake:

title: drop model class from additional responses when generating openapi

The `"model"` key is not dropped when generating `openapi.json`, and an error occurs when encoding the class into json.

Thanks! :rocket: 

Do you guys think "crush" is understandable? I just updated the Spanish version to use "la persona que te gusta", which is kinda long :disappointed: but there's no better alternative.

I don't know if in Portuguese there's a better word for that, would people normally say "crush"? Or would it be better to make it something like "a pessoa da que você gosta" or similar?

I also added a lot of emojis of :heart_eyes: to later refer to that person as "esa persona :heart_eyes: " instead of writing the whole thing again.

Maybe we could do the same for the Portuguese version?

Here's the new Spanish version: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/docs/es/docs/async.md

> Thanks! 
> 
> Do you guys think "crush" is understandable? I just updated the Spanish version to use "la persona que te gusta", which is kinda long  but there's no better alternative.

> 
> Maybe we could do the same for the Portuguese version?
> 
> Here's the new Spanish version: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/docs/es/docs/async.md

Hey @tiangolo , here in Brazil it's common use some words in english mixing with portuguese sentences and expressions. `Crush` is a word that is cool and natural nowadays. We have a word in portuguese that translates perfectly crush, `paquera`. But it is so old, kkkkk. I wil talk with @cassiobotaro  and @marcosmmb to see if we keep `crush` or replace with a portuguese word that reflects the exact meaning. And about the emoji  :heart_eyes:  is amazing.
Thanks for the notes.
Cheers
I think we should keep _crush_ in italic. As you said, "is a word that is cool and natural nowadays".
Awesome, sounds great with _crush_ then. :rocket:

Thanks @Serrones for your contribution! 🚀

And thanks @cassiobotaro and @lsglucas for the reviews! :cake:

```suggestion
Se sua aplicação (de alguma forma) não tem que se comunicar com nada mais e tem que esperar que o respondam, use `async def`.
```

I might refactor the rest of the docs to include bcrypt as well.

Hello Camila
In my mind it was finished.
I'll check
I think that maybe I didn't run the markdown parser to verify how it outputs the html

for instance, when it says **!!!check**

And after sending the pull request I realized that wasn't meant to be translated.

But I am very open to check and finish if you please point out what is still undone.

y donde decía !!! note , puse !!! nota

creo que es lo único que falta

si me ayudas a verifcar eso, lo dejo listo y podría seguir traduciendo otras páginas
y perdon la primiparada en estos temas
gracias

title: Allow to add OpenAPI tag descriptions
body:
This pull requests addresses #566 .
It adds an additional parameter to FastAPI `openapi_tags` which allow to add tags descriptions to OpenAPI and the automatic API docs UIs.

title: Better JSON Decode error handling
body:
I was annoyed by the fact that if JSON parsing fails for the body you receive HTTP Exception 400.
I think RequestValidationError is a better suit for the case, for the client to realize what have he done wrong exactly (position of wrong character) and body to be part of the exception (so it can be logged)

title: Fix callable class generator dependencies
body:
Fixes/related to #1204 

Resolving dependencies uses `inspect.isgeneratorfunction` and `inspect.isasyncgenfunction` which match async methods and functions, but not a class (or other callable) that has an async generator `__call__` method.

It also adds 4 new test cases to ensure that dependencies work as expected for class methods that yield instead of return values.

@mrosales  @tiangolo 

Hello, may I ask if there are any specific considerations for replacing `asyncio.iscoroutinefunction` with `inspect.iscoroutinefunction`?

In Python version 3.9, this change will prevent asynchronous functions compiled with Cython from executing correctly, as `inspect.iscoroutinefunction(cythonized_async_function)` will return `False`. https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/2273#issuecomment-1188664872

Is it possible to revert this method back to asyncio.iscoroutinefunction? If possible, I will submit a pull request.

title: Replace 400 codes with 401 in security tutorial
body:
[From this](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/32752578/whats-the-appropriate-http-status-code-to-return-if-a-user-tries-logging-in-wit) it looks like 401 is more appropriate.

Feel free to close if you think 400 is better (ie. to emphasize it can be customized).

Side note: Looks like PRs are piling up. Just an idea, perhaps you would benefit from giving more people merge access so you don't get bored of all these tiny docs PRs. Something I read once talked about someone's philosophy of giving repo write access to every single contributor since they argued this would increase the quality of contributions and bad actors are very sparse. Of course this wouldn't be wise in a larger repository like this, but I think it puts the risk of bad actors/suboptimal code contributions versus PR suffocation into perspective.

> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1370?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [4e77737...500e01f](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1370?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).

Thanks! The problem is that a 401 _must_ also return a `WWW-Authenticate` header with a "valid challenge".

So, for example, when using the integrated HTTPBasic or OAuth2 utilities, that's what they do.

But if someone returns that code, they would also have to make sure to return a valid header with a valid challenge. That could be cumbersome in many cases as it's not obvious what to put there.

On the other side, a 400 error that describes a "generic" client error is still valid.

So I'm gonna pass on this, but thanks for your effort! :cake:

title: Added test for repeating cookies in response headers
body: Found this issue using fastapi.  I haven't tracked down the cause.  It seems every additional layer of redirection doubles the cookies in the header.  No clue if this would actually break anything, but it seems like incorrect behavior at any rate.
comments:
the problem is that a call to `solve_dependencies` shares a [single `Response`](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L440-L446
) with [its recursive calls for child dependencies](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L472-L491), which may cause the original to have its `headers.raw` extended redundantly:
```
        solved_result = await solve_dependencies(
            ...
            response=response,
            ...
        )
        (
            ...
            sub_response,
            ...
        ) = solved_result
        sub_response = cast(Response, sub_response)
        response.headers.raw.extend(sub_response.headers.raw)
        if sub_response.status_code:
            response.status_code = sub_response.status_code
```
one obvious solution that comes to mind is to check whether `sub_response is not response` before using it to update `response`

Thanks for discovering this and creating the tests @scottsmith2gmail ! :detective: :bug: :heavy_check_mark: 

I just merged the fix by @obataku including your tests, so we can close this one. :rocket:

title: Bugfix for duplicated headers from indirect dependencies
body:
addresses the issue discovered by @scottsmith2gmail in #1385 and adds the test he proposed

> It seems every additional layer of redirection doubles the cookies in the header.

title: add pydantic pycharm plugin in document
body:
@tiangolo 
Thank you for writing excellent documents.

I'm developing PyCharm Plugin for pydantic.
The plugin provides features that are auto-completion, type-checking, refactoring, searching, inspections for pydantic.
The PR adds the link of the plugin in FastAPI's document.

Also, the link is in pydantic documents.
https://pydantic-docs.helpmanual.io/pycharm_plugin/

You feel free to ping me. I will help to support pydantic for VS Code. :nerd_face: 
If pydantic will be more popular then, I can send a PR to VS Code repo. :+1: 

Btw, I always use FastAPI tutorial to teach new employees to develop web applications.
Thank you very much :smile: :rocket:

> because I know how do we analyze pydnatic models.

That's right! I see your plugin has a lot of code with all the magic :sparkles: 

---

I don't really know what would be the best approach for a VS Code plugin for Pydantic, but I think maybe trying to re-use parts of [Pyright](https://github.com/microsoft/pyright) could work.

Or maybe trying to use something like https://github.com/Instagram/LibCST combined with a "language server" and a layer on top in an extension... I don't really know, just random ideas :sweat_smile: 

> You feel free to ping me. I will help to support pydantic for VS Code.

Thank you! :bow: 

> Btw, I always use FastAPI tutorial to teach new employees to develop web applications.
> Thank you very much

That's awesome! I'm glad it's being useful :smile:

title: 🐛 Support custom OpenAPI / JSON Schema fields in the generated output OpenAPI
body:
Pydantic supports adding custom fields other than `example` and `examples` using `BaseModel.Config.schema_extra`. This change ensures that those extra fields exist in the FastAPI generated Open API schema as well.

This is in line with what the current docs say:
> Other info
> The same way, you could add your own custom extra info that would be added to the JSON Schema for each model, for example to customize a frontend user interface, etc.

(https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/schema-extra-example/#other-info)

Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments?utm_medium=referral&utm_source=github&utm_content=comment&utm_campaign=pr+comments&utm_term=Sebasti%C3%A1n+Ram%C3%ADrez).
📝 Docs preview for commit 3c20f2323b0b3fdff4e316f9dbc0ccafb75ada5d at: https://60e098eaae1c69f28f225bd6--fastapi.netlify.app

Good point, thanks @jmagnusson! Thanks for your contribution! :coffee: 

And thanks for the patience :sweat_smile:

Are custom fields supported by the [OpenAPI spec](https://swagger.io/specification/)?

I saw breakages using [OpenAPI generator](https://openapi-generator.tech/)'s schema validation when custom fields started appearing in the generated schema starting in [FastAPI 0.66.0](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/releases/tag/0.66.0), which includes this PR.

A minimal reproducible example here: https://github.com/collerek/ormar/issues/291

> Are custom fields supported by the [OpenAPI spec](https://swagger.io/specification/)?

Yes, see Specification Extensions here: https://swagger.io/specification/. All `x-` prefixed keys are allowed (though they mentioned that not all tooling supports it.)

> I saw breakages using [OpenAPI generator](https://openapi-generator.tech/)'s schema validation when custom fields started appearing in the generated schema starting in [FastAPI 0.66.0](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/releases/tag/0.66.0), which includes this PR.

We ran into this when just using some pydantic `Field` arguments (e.g. `const`) that now get erroneously added to the OpenAPI schema. I raised #3745 on this if it needs to be discussed; if I can get some consensus that it is a bug then I am happy to try and fix it.

title: [WIP] Optionally skip json_encoder for orjson & response_model_skip_validation
body:
This PR is to address optimizations noted in #1359 

Done:
* [x] Add a `response_model_skip_validation` parameter that is to be set on a per-request basis. This will skip validation if `response_model` is set.
* [x] Skip `jsonable_encoder` if the `Response` has `skip_jsonable_encoder = True` set and various other conditions pass.
  We set this for `orjson` because as per https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1224#issuecomment-617243856 it appears to handle many common python datatypes. We added `decimal.Decimal` as that was the only one missing.
* [x] If a `response_model` has been specified we require `response_model_skip_validation` before allowing skipping of `jsonable_encoder`, as it skips coercion.
  As the user just said they know what they are doing. Right?
* [ ] Allow skipping validation globally via configuring the app.
* [ ] Update docs

Motivation is based on benchmarks (more details at #1359) I performed.
Some conclusions are:
* Skipping `jsonable_encoder` can increase performance by over 35% (probably more if no `response_model` has been specified.
* Skipping validation can increase performance by 30-50%
* Skipping both can increase performance by 140% (https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1359#issuecomment-622998691 provides an explanation)

After trying to write tests for this I realise that my assumptions didn't take into account non-complete or non-coerced responses.

Seems that if the response is already a pydantic object it qualifies for the jsonable_encoder to be skipped. (I did tests on this, and it seems to hold)

Looking at the pydantic `ModelField` it's not apparent how to test if the input is of type in the ModelField, of it it is a list/dict.

Seems like we can only do this skipping if validation is turned off for the response?
Hi @grigi and @tiangolo ! Do we have any update here? Looking forward to this for a long time!

Hi, I forgot about this sometime during having covid. Thanks for reminding me about this.

@tiangolo I have a few questions:

Assumptions (please correct me if I'm wrong):
* A response can either be a native Pydantic model object(`PMO`), OR a `dict`, OR a manual `Response` type.
* We have no control over a manual `Response` type.
* I'm assuming that `dict` responses will want to be validated and turned into a `PMO` before serialised by default. There is cases where the user wants to say "trust me" and turn off validation, and we should allow this with a global setting.

Questions:
* Can we assume that a native `PMO` is already validated? (You have to jump through serious loops to build an unvalidated `PMO`, and if you do that, one can argue that it's your perogative to ensure that things are correct). If so, can we safely skip the validation on `PMO`'s by default?
* Why did you replace the JSON encoder with your own one, instead of using the provided one from Pydantic: https://pydantic-docs.helpmanual.io/usage/exporting_models/#modeljson ? It already seems to handle all the common data type conversions correctly? Based on quick tests, it appears to be faster too.


Depending on the answer you provide, I can complete this PR. It would be lovely to have a faster response type for the cleaner code by default.

Right now returning a `PMO` (which is the cleanest in code) will result in the slowest response type.
`dict`'s are slightly faster, and `Response` is the fastest.

I would like making `PMO` perform better by default, skip validation and re-building of `PMO`, so we can just go to serialisation directly?

I would like to make the default `JSONResponse` use the provided `PMO.json()` if available so we can honour custom encoders on it.

I would like to make the `ORJSONResponse` allow skipping the datatype conversion by default, as it seems to handle all the common cases as well.

BUT, I would like to limit breakage. So what would be safe behaviour be for `PMO` and `dict` response types?

fastAPI is an API framework doing data validation , so it's the default behavior -> https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1792#issuecomment-665165674

if you want skip validation for performance purposes then it should be explicit The documentation already explain perfectly this :

https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/response-directly/

`It won't do any data conversion with Pydantic models, it won't convert the contents to any type, etc.`

It's nice that FastAPI does data validation, but it's best done in development and testing environments only. 
In production, validating APIs that return lists of objects is a significant performance penalty

> Hi, I forgot about this sometime during having covid. Thanks for reminding me about this.
> 
> @tiangolo I have a few questions:
> 
> Assumptions (please correct me if I'm wrong):
> 
> * A response can either be a native Pydantic model object(`PMO`), OR a `dict`, OR a manual `Response` type.
> * We have no control over a manual `Response` type.
> * I'm assuming that `dict` responses will want to be validated and turned into a `PMO` before serialised by default. There is cases where the user wants to say "trust me" and turn off validation, and we should allow this with a global setting.
> 
> Questions:
> 
> * Can we assume that a native `PMO` is already validated? (You have to jump through serious loops to build an unvalidated `PMO`, and if you do that, one can argue that it's your perogative to ensure that things are correct). If so, can we safely skip the validation on `PMO`'s by default?
> * Why did you replace the JSON encoder with your own one, instead of using the provided one from Pydantic: https://pydantic-docs.helpmanual.io/usage/exporting_models/#modeljson ? It already seems to handle all the common data type conversions correctly? Based on quick tests, it appears to be faster too.
> 
> Depending on the answer you provide, I can complete this PR. It would be lovely to have a faster response type for the cleaner code by default.

Based on @tiangolo's answer here: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1107#issuecomment-612963659, it seems that the pydantic json utility was created after the json_encoder implementation in FastAPI. It would be a great improvement to replace the current json_encoder with pydantic's own utility.
@grigi Any update on this? I’d love to be able to disable heavyweight response validation in production.

@lslunis Unfortunately due to not getting answers from FastAPI developers I abandoned the project. I still believe that my findings were valid and the proposal made sense, but its more than a year later, and things might have changed.

Anyone is welcome to take this work and continue/reimplement it.

title: Cannot import ''OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict''
body:
Fixes an import error:     

from fastapi.security import OAuth2PasswordBearer, OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict
ImportError: cannot import name 'OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict'


Currently using the workaround:
from fastapi.security.oauth2 import OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict

Not sure whether the maintainers intended for the import to be different from the rest of the security imports. I assume this is a simple oversight.

> You may also want to change the corresponding documentation as well.

I would but I don't know what corresponds.
https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/search?q=OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict
I always found it weird that the strict version was not exported, I think this is a good improvement. :+1:

Thanks for your contribution @RichardHoekstra ! :rocket: 

So, it was actually intentional, I would expect (and suggest) everyone to use the non-strict version, as the strict version enforces clients to send a constant value doesn't add much, as it's just a constant.

Because of that, I didn't expose it directly.

But... I think this is like the 3th PR suggesting that change :joy: 

So I guess it's gonna be more intuitive if it's just there even though I don't expect many people to use it.

Thanks for the review @phy25 ! :bow:

title: 🐛 Fix Enum handling with their own schema definitions
body:
🐛 Fix Enum handling with their own schema definitions.

I missed an error in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1461 , the story is a bit more complex. This currently breaks Swagger UI when using enums in other parts apart from bodies (e.g. path params).

It's necessary to add enums to the whole handling with OpenAPI of models to include the new definition in the components.

These changes plus a couple of updates in https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/pull/1432 work correctly in Swagger UI and generate an independent schema for enums (which was the original intention).

title: Remove HTTPDigest as it's not helpful
body:
Hey,

I removed `fastapi.security.HTTPDigest` as it is not standard compliant (see [RFC 7616](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7616)), very confusing for users and not useful or helpful at all. E.g. `auto_error` raises `HTTP_403_FORBIDDEN` which is not part of the standard and also misses any required `WWW-Authenticate` headers. Due to the complexity when generating `WWW-Authenticate` (i.e. a good - oftentimes even application dependent - nonce) and validating the corresponding `Authorization` header (e.g. nonces really used once) I would suggest to leave the implementation details up to the user and not giving him (including me when I first looked at FastAPI) a false sense of support by "just passing on a string".

As no one else raised an issue yet, usage seems to be quite low anyways...

Merging this would close #1476

title: Remove HTTPBase as it is not used anywhere
body:
Hey,

while it was looking at `fastapi.security.http`, I noticed that `HTTPBase` is not used anywhere and creates an unnecessary level of complexity:

- models inheriting from `HTTPBase` do never call `super` and overwrite all properties themselves
- `fastapi.security` does not expose `HTTPBase`
- the docs do not mention it
- it's not really helpful as most non-"Basic" authentication schemes (see [IANA](https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-authschemes/http-authschemes.xhtml)) require much more complex `auto_error` handling than `HTTPBase` can provide
- `HTTPBasic.__call__` is incompatible with `HTTPBase.__call__` which is really bad in a typed context and requires a `#type: ignore`

Therefore I hereby propose to remove `HTTPBase`.

title: squash __root__ in BaseModel json encoding
body:
Fixes #911.

This corrects the specific problem described by @peku33, where the response is received by the client as `{"__root__": foo}`. This change simply converts to `foo` during json encoding.

Note that these `__root__` structures may still exist internally, e.g. `{"foo": {"__root__": foo}}`. That is a pydantic bug (https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/issues/1414) with an open PR.
comments:

The idea there was actually to show a minimum example of how it could be done. Keeping the focus on the API part, and keeping the HTML as simple as possible.

I would actually expect users to use a framework like React or Vue.js for frontend instead of plain JS, but I wouldn't want to "enforce" it in the docs, the same way, I would prefer not to add very complex examples that I would expect developers to implement in a different way anyway.

So I'll pass on this. But thanks for your effort! :cake:

my idea was same + adding more basic and simple funtionality
I am a back-end developer
my job is to deliver a simple working html to UI guy.
I did it for myself so I thanked this would be beneficial to others

Yep, get it, I would still expect it to vary a lot from team to team.

Nevertheless, it could be a good idea for a blog post! :memo:

title: 📝 Update help and templates
body:
📝 Update help and issue templates.

## Remove the "bug" template

I'm removing the "bug" template. From all the issues reported as "bug" only a very low percentage were actual bugs.

Many were problems (bugs) in user's code, some were actually feature requests, some were misunderstandings of FastAPI, Python, Pydantic, the standards (HTTP, JSON, etc), some were bugs or feature requests for the other libraries (Pydantic, Swagger UI), etc.

So, I'm simplifying it, making "Question or Problem" a single template where people can ask questions and talk about their problems, and then, if necessary, I can label them as bugs after confirming there is something actually wrong in FastAPI.

## Require better question structure

With the current very "free form" question template many questions end up with incomplete code examples copied from internal apps, which makes them impossible to debug. Or very vague questions that also make them difficult to answer.

Having a more structured template, requiring a self-contained, [minimal, reproducible, example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/minimal-reproducible-example) will make it a lot easier to run and see the actual problem and a lot easier to help others.

At the same time, it will force users to isolate and think the question/problem thoroughly, and that could help them find their own problem themselves (that happens to me all the time when "writing" a new issue for some project, right before realizing where I had the error in my code, right before submitting the new issue :sweat_smile: ).

## Requiring help in exchange

Some times, some people ask very loose questions or questions that had an answer that was very easy to find by just looking into the docs or doing a search.

By requiring a compromise to give (a very little) back to the community, helping others, it should on one side make people want to solve the problem themselves before having to make a compromise to put effort to find someone else to help. And on the other side, it should also encourage others to come and help.

There's a huge effort done by a bunch of community members that come here to answer issues and help others, I really appreciate it :tada: :cake: 

And I think it would be great if more people came to help a little bit and shared the effort of helping others. :rocket:

title: Add __all__ definitions to main fastapi module
body:
Added __all__ constraints to the main fastapi __init__.py file, to:
* Fixes https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1309
* Enable `implicit_reexport = False` configuration value to be set in mypy configuration.
* Limit what gets imported with `form fastapi import *`

> `Δ = absolute <relative> (impact)`, `ø = not affected`, `? = missing data`
> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1537?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [543ef77...1ce19c9](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1537?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).

Thanks @Kludex this is a good suggestion.
I'll extend the test with this check.

I also have to add some submodule __all__ definitions, cause of recursive nesting.

Converted the PR to draft for these reasons.

@Kludex 
I need some help with this, most of the starlette reexports are done via importing it to a module, and than reexporting from the main fastapi module. The only exception is the StaticFiles class. Is there any design decision behind this, or is it just by mistake?
My solution now reexports the StaticFiles class as all the others via the fastapi init, and also implements __all__ for the fastapi.staticfiles module, to not break the existing public API.
But it introduces a new public export `from fastapi import StaticFiles` .

I think this is more consistent this way, without breaking existing code.
What do you think?

If you do what you're doing, we'll be able to import TestClient from fastapi i.e. `from fastapi import TestClient`. I don't know the decisions made about the allowance, but I think that's not what we want. I'm not sure if works, but can you check:
```
from . import testclient

__all__ = [
   ...
   "testclient",
   ...
]
``` 
I haven't tested, so I don't know if works, you need to try. I'm too tired now, see you tomorrow.

You are correct, my changes make the `from fastapi import TestClient` a valid import. Even for mypy.
If we don`t want that, which is also fine, then it the __all__ definition containing `TestClient` should stay in the `testclient.py` file. Nothing else is needed, we don't have to import every module to the top level package.

I raised the question, because I didn`t see the logic behind, what starlette reexports are done from the top level package, and what are done in submodules.

To compare:

Top level: `status, BackgroundTasks, Request, Response, WebSocket`

Submodule: `StaticFiles, Jiinja2Templates, TestClient, FileResponse, HTMLResponse, JsonResponse ... in responses.py, WebSocketDisconnect`

There is a bit of mix here, like for responses. If it is intentional, than is fine too, than __all__ should be added to the reexports in the `responses.py`.  This also clarifies how the imports should be used to the package users.
@Kludex should we work on getting this PR merged? I don't want to be pushy on this, I know there are more important issues open.
Is just that I don't know what we want. Maybe @tiangolo 's awesome inputs can help here. 💪

Thanks for your effort @gjedlicska ! :nerd_face: :coffee: 

And thanks for the discussion here @Kludex ! :nerd_face: :memo: 

I actually prefer not to have the `__all__` as it adds code duplication that can get out of sync easily. I would prefer `import x as x`, as any errors in that would be detected by the editor.

Completely understand, it can be a burden to maintain. I'm doing it on a few projects, and to work around the no editor support, my solution is a small test function, that checks the public modules in __all__ against what is imported into __init__.py for any discrepancies either direction.

Anyhow mypy can work both ways. 
Thanks for your reply.
I'm closing this.

https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1309

This should be closed aswell.
review_comments:
```suggestion
def test_fastapi_module_exports():
"""
Test the publicly exported module set.
Benefits for declaring explicitly the public exports:
    * Using __all__ sets what gets imported with `from fastapi import *`
    * Enables mypy to pass all checks with fastapi when using `implicit_reexport = False` 
"""
import yourpackage

missing = set(n for n in yourpackage.__all__
              if getattr(yourpackage, n, None) is None)
self.assertEmpty(missing, msg=f"__all__ contains unresolved names: {missing}")
```
Reason: You're not checking if something else is being imported. 
Reference: http://xion.io/post/code/python-all-wild-imports.html
I don't know how to suggest a whole block of code, sorry :(
this is a valid point. I'll add it a bit later. I also have to work on some submodule __all__ definitions

```Python
class TestParams:
```

```Python
def test_depends_repr_empty():

```

title: feat: add servers option for OpenAPI
body:
Closes/related to #872

The missing server option has been a drawback when it comes to the swagger UI (e.g. provide prod and test servers) and portability of the openapi.json to services that is based on knowing the servers.

Example usage:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.openapi.models import Server

server1 = Server(url="http://example.com", description="optional description")
server2 = Server(url="http://test.com")

app = FastAPI(servers=[server1, server2])

@app.get("/")
async def root():
    return {"message": "Hello World"}
```

It would also work to just pass a dictionary, but then the typechecker won't be happy:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI(servers=[{"url": "http://example.com", "description": "test"}])
```
